26: The Ethiopian eunuch: Ethiopia (Aithiopia) in Greek-Roman geography referred to the Nubian kingdom south of Egypt (modern Sudan); the Candace was the queen-mother title of the ruling dynasty (Meroitic ktke; several queens held this title historically — Strabo 17.1.54; Pliny NH 6.186). As court treasurer (dynastes) he held significant administrative power. As a eunuch he was excluded from full Jewish assembly participation (Deuteronomy 23:1) but Isaiah 56:3-8 had promised eunuchs a 'monument and a name better than sons and daughters'. Philip's baptism of him is the first recorded fulfillment of that Isaianic promise: the Spirit-led inclusion of the excluded.
1: Saul's approval of Stephen's execution signals his role as an official enforcer — not a mob participant but a legal representative of the Sanhedrin's authority. The dispersion (diaspora) of the Jerusalem church echoes the Assyrian and Babylonian dispersions of Israel; Luke's use of the same concept transforms what appears to be catastrophe into the mechanism of the gospel's spread. Only the apostles remain — whether because they were protected by community respect (cf. Acts 5:26) or because they understood themselves as called to Jerusalem is not explained.
2: Devout men (eulabeis andres) bury Stephen and mourn loudly — kopetos megas (great lamentation) is the vocabulary of formal Jewish mourning. Torah required burial of the executed (Deuteronomy 21:23); these men risk association with a condemned criminal by performing the rite. Stephen's burial and mourning contrasts with Saul's persecution: even in the moment of suppression, the community maintains its covenantal obligations.
3: Saul's house-to-house search (kata tous oikous eisporeuomenos) targeting both men and women reflects the synagogue network of Jerusalem: Christian communities met in homes, and Saul's enforcement required knowing which households were centers of the movement. The dragging (surōn) into prison is violent, public, and indiscriminate by gender — a detail Luke carries as evidence of the persecution's severity before the Damascus reversal.
4: The scattered believers preach the word everywhere (diēlthon euangelizomenoi ton logon): the dispersion achieves the opposite of what the persecutors intended. The Jerusalem church had been stationary for several years; the persecution provides the external pressure that activates the Acts 1:8 geographic expansion. The believers who scatter are not apostles (Acts 8:1) but ordinary disciples — the Pentecostal democratization of prophetic witness now extends to everyone in the community.
5: Philip goes to the city of Samaria (or 'a city of Samaria'). The Samaritans were the religiously mixed population left after the Assyrian resettlement of the northern kingdom (2 Kgs 17); they maintained their own version of the Torah and worshipped at Mount Gerizim rather than Jerusalem. Jews and Samaritans had deep mutual hostility (John 4:9 — 'Jews have no dealings with Samaritans'). Philip's Samaritan mission crosses a centuries-old ethnic-religious boundary that Jesus had already foreshadowed (Luke 10:33; John 4).
6: The crowds paid attention to what Philip said when they heard and saw the signs he did — the combination of authoritative proclamation and miraculous attestation is the standard Lukan pattern (Acts 2:22; Acts 14:3). Homothymadon proseirchon (with one accord paying attention) echoes the unanimity of the Jerusalem community at Pentecost; the Samaritan community is drawn into the same Spirit-movement with similar communal response.
7: Unclean spirits crying out as they depart: the exorcism ministry in Samaria continues the pattern of Jesus's own Galilean ministry (Luke 4:33-37; Acts 6:18). The same formula — spirits coming out 'crying with a loud voice' — appears in the synoptic exorcisms. The healings of the paralyzed and lame echo Acts 3 (Peter's temple healing). Philip's signs replicate both Jesus's own signs and Peter's earlier ones, establishing a consistent pattern of authenticated mission.
8: Great joy in the city (chara megalē): the Samaritan city's joy echoes the joy that characterized Jesus's mission (Luke 2:10; Acts 15:7, 10; Acts 24:52) and the Jerusalem community (Acts 2:46). Joy is Luke's signature response to salvation; its presence among Samaritans confirms that the Spirit's work is not ethnically bounded. The joy of Samaria will be reported back to Jerusalem (Acts 8:14) as evidence that the gospel has genuinely crossed the cultural barrier.
9: Simon the Magician (mageuōn) — the verb is from mageia, the technical term for the professional practice of spells, divination, and wonder-working common throughout the ancient Mediterranean. 'Magician' in this period was not pejorative in all circles; professional magic-practitioners (magoi) were well-paid consultants in Hellenistic and Roman contexts. Simon's claim to be 'someone great' (legōn einai tina heauton megan) is the self-promotion style of Hellenistic wonder-workers competing for patronage and reputation.
10: 'This man is the power of God that is called Great' (hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē): the crowd's designation of Simon reflects syncretistic language blending Jewish divine-power vocabulary with Hellenistic wonder-worker titulature. Dynamis (power) is a Jewish term for divine energy (cf. 'the Power' as a circumlocution for God in Matthew 26:64; Mark 14:62). Calling Simon 'the Great Power of God' shows the Samaritan community applying Jewish theological vocabulary to authenticate a Hellenistic magician — the syncretism Philip's mission will displace.
11: Simon had amazed the Samarians for a long time with magic (eksestathenai autous tais mageiais hikanon chronon): existēmi (amaze/astonish) is used here for the effect of magic as it elsewhere describes the effect of miracles (Acts 2:7, 12; Acts 8:9, 13). Luke's use of the same reaction-word is deliberate: the crowd cannot distinguish magic from miracle by affective response alone; what distinguishes them is the source and the content.
12: When they believed Philip preaching about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women: the baptism formula here is 'the name of Jesus Christ' rather than the Trinitarian formula of later Christian practice. The explicit inclusion of women (gynaikēs) in the baptismal list is a Lukan social marker — the community that forms around the gospel includes those marginalized by both Jewish and Hellenistic social hierarchies.
13: Simon himself believed and was baptized (episteusen kai baptistheis): Luke records Simon's apparent conversion without qualification. The later episode (Acts 8:18-24) will reveal the shallowness of that conversion; but at this point Luke presents the same language as for other conversions. Ancient commentators (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) made Simon Magus the founder of Christian Gnosticism; Acts' portrait is more ambiguous — a man who received the sacrament but not the grace it signified.
14: The Jerusalem apostles send Peter and John to Samaria when they hear that Samaria has received the word: the apostolic visitation functions as quality-control and communal legitimation. The Jerusalem church was the recognized center; the Samaritan conversion needed apostolic ratification to prevent schism. The sending of Peter and John — the two senior apostles, both named — signals the seriousness with which Jerusalem receives the Samaritan mission report.
15: Peter and John prayed for the Samaritans to receive the Holy Spirit (hopōs labōsin pneuma hagion): the prayer for Spirit-reception implies that water-baptism had occurred without the Spirit's manifest arrival. This sequence — baptism then Spirit — is anomalous in Acts (contrast Acts 10:44-47 where Spirit precedes baptism; Acts 19:5-6 where Spirit follows baptism immediately). The anomaly may reflect the Samaritans' liminal status: full incorporation required both the community's baptismal rite and the apostolic confirmation of the Spirit's acceptance.
16: 'For he had not yet fallen on any of them; they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus' — the Spirit's non-arrival despite valid baptism is Luke's deliberate puzzle. Various theological explanations have been offered: deficiency in faith, the need for apostolic mediation, or a unique dispensational moment in the church's expansion. Luke's concern is the Spirit's role as the divine confirmation of the gospel's geographic expansion: each major crossing of a boundary (Jew → Samaritan → Gentile) receives its own Spirit-certification.
17: They laid their hands on them and they received the Holy Spirit: the laying on of hands (epithesis tōn cheirōn) is a Jewish commissioning gesture (Numbers 27:18-23 — Moses laying hands on Joshua) repurposed as a channel of Spirit-transmission in the early church. Its use here by apostles specifically ties the Samaritan reception to the Jerusalem apostolic community. The visible manifestation that followed (which Simon saw, Acts 8:18) presumably resembled what had happened at Pentecost.
18: Simon offered money (prosenegken autois chrēmata) for the ability to transmit the Spirit: the offer reflects his professional framework — in Hellenistic magic, extraordinary abilities could be purchased from established practitioners. The term 'simony' (selling church offices or spiritual gifts) derives from this episode. Simon misunderstands the gift as a transferable technique; his commercial approach applies the logic of the patronage economy to what is, by definition, God's sovereign gift.
19: 'Give me also this authority (exousian tautēn)': Simon wants the apostolic authority to confer the Spirit-gift, not just to receive it. His request reveals the category error: he is operating in a system where spiritual power hierarchies are purchasable from those higher up. Peter's response refuses the transaction entirely and reframes the economy: the gift of God (dōrea tou theou) cannot be obtained with silver.
20: 'Your silver perish with you' (to argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian): a formula of imprecation — the same doom for the man and his money together. Apōleia (destruction) is the NT word for eschatological judgment (Matthew 7:13; Philippians 1:28). Peter's severity reflects the gravity of the offense: attempting to corrupt the channel of divine grace by commercial transaction is not merely an error but a fundamental corruption of the gospel's gift-character.
21: 'You have no share or portion in this matter' (ouk estin soi meris oud klēros en tō logō toutō): meris (share) and klēros (lot/inheritance) are the OT vocabulary for covenantal participation — the land portions of the twelve tribes (Josh 14-19) and the Levitical 'lot' in God himself. Peter declares that Simon is outside the covenant community despite his baptism; his 'heart is not right before God' (ouk estin eutheia hē kardia sou enanti tou theou).
22: 'Repent therefore of this wickedness of yours (apo tēs kakias sou tautēs) and pray to the Lord that, if possible, the intent of your heart may be forgiven you' — Peter's conditional offer of forgiveness ('if possible') is remarkably hesitant. The 'intent of the heart' (epinoian tēs kardias sou) — the formulated plan, the considered proposal — is what requires forgiveness: Simon had calculated the commercial transaction. The condition may reflect genuine uncertainty about whether Simon's repentance would be sincere given the depth of his heart's orientation.
23: 'You are in the gall of bitterness and in the bond of iniquity' (eis cholēn pikrias kai syndesmon adikias horō se onta): the gall of bitterness echoes Deuteronomy 29:18 LXX — the root of bitterness that produces apostasy. The bond of iniquity is Isaiah 58:6 LXX — the chains of wickedness from which Israel is to be freed. Peter diagnoses Simon's condition with the vocabulary of the covenantal curse: he is in the state that precedes Israel's exile and judgment, not the state of the gospel's liberation.
24: Simon's response — 'Pray for me to the Lord, that nothing of what you have said may come upon me' — is ambiguous: is it genuine repentance or merely fear of judgment? He asks the apostles to pray rather than praying himself; he focuses on avoiding punishment rather than expressing sorrow for the act. Second-century traditions developed Simon into the archheretic of early Christianity (Justin Martyr, Apol. 1.26; Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 1.23), but Luke leaves his fate unresolved.
25: Peter and John returned to Jerusalem preaching to many Samaritan villages (kōmas tōn Samaritōn euēngelizonto): on the way back they extend the mission through the villages — the apostles who came to verify and confirm the Samaritan mission end up themselves extending it. The Jerusalem-Samaria boundary, so significant in Jewish social geography, is dissolved by the apostolic mission itself. The Samaritans are now part of the one community that Peter and John oversee.
27: The Ethiopian eunuch was a court official (dynastēs) of the Candace queen of the Ethiopians, in charge of her treasury (epi pasēs tēs gazēs autēs). Candace was a title, not a personal name (Greek writers including Strabo knew of Candace of Meroe). The Ethiopian kingdom of Meroe (modern Sudan) was known to Greek and Roman writers as a distant, prestigious civilization. Eunuchs could hold high administrative positions in Hellenistic courts; they were also excluded from 'the assembly of the LORD' in Deuteronomy 23:1 — a tension Isaiah 56:3-5 directly addresses with a promise of inclusion.
28: The eunuch was reading aloud from the prophet Isaiah as he returned in his chariot. Ancient literacy was auditory — silent reading was rare and notable (Augustine describes Ambrose's silent reading as remarkable, Conf. 6.3). Reading aloud in a moving vehicle suggests either a high literacy level (being able to maintain focus) or a desire to hear the text. His possession of an Isaiah scroll indicates both personal religious devotion and substantial wealth — a scroll was expensive to commission.
29: 'The Spirit said to Philip, Go over and join this chariot' (proselthen kai kollēthēti tō harmatī toutō): the Spirit's direct instruction links Philip's encounter to the series of divine directives in Acts (Acts 10:19-20; Acts 13:2; Acts 16:6-10). Kollaō (join/cling to) is the word for intimate association — the same word in Luke 15:15 for the prodigal son attaching himself to a citizen, and in 1 Corinthians 6:16-17 for sexual and spiritual union. Philip is to make immediate, close contact — not a casual inquiry but a full engagement.
30: 'Do you understand what you are reading?' (ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis): the Greek wordplay — ginōskeis... anaginōskeis (understand... read) — may be Luke's own Greek or may reflect the actual conversation in Greek. The eunuch's response — 'how can I, unless someone guides me?' — reflects the standard assumption of Jewish hermeneutics: Scripture requires an authorized interpreter. The Qumran pesher texts (1QpHab) operate on the same assumption — the text's meaning is disclosed through a moreh hatsedek (Teacher of Righteousness), someone with interpretive authority.
31: 'He invited Philip to come up and sit with him' (parekAlesen ton Philippon anabanta kathisai syn autō): the eunuch takes the initiative — his question (Acts 8:30) has identified Philip as a potential guide, and he acts on that identification with social decisiveness. His invitation is also a social levelling: a court treasurer inviting a travelling preacher to share his chariot is a status inversion that the episode's whole arc embodies — the insider of the Candace's court is an outsider from Israel's assembly (Deuteronomy 23:1), and the gospel dissolves the distinction.
32: The passage he was reading — Isaiah 53:7-8 — is the heart of the fourth Servant Song. 'As a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth' evokes the image of passive, voiceless suffering — the Servant endures judgment without self-defence. In Jewish interpretation, the Servant figure had multiple identifications: Israel corporately, an idealized Israel, a specific prophet. The eunuch's question 'of whom does the prophet say this?' (Acts 8:34) reflects the hermeneutical openness of a thoughtful reader who knows the text is about someone specific but does not know who.
33: Isaiah 53:8 — 'In his humiliation his judgment was taken away. Who can describe his generation? For his life is taken away from the earth' — the LXX of this verse is notably different from the Hebrew MT, and it is the LXX Philip would have been reading with the eunuch. The LXX's 'his judgment was taken away in his humiliation' emphasizes the injustice of the verdict; 'who will describe his generation?' becomes a meditation on the mysterious identity of this suffering figure. The text invites interpretation.
34: 'Tell me, about whom does the prophet say this — about himself or about someone else?' (peri heautou ē peri heterou tinos): this hermeneutical question mirrors the contemporary Jewish debate about whether Isa 53 referred to Isaiah himself, to Israel collectively, or to the coming Messiah. The targum on Isaiah interpreted the Servant as the Messiah but removed the suffering to Israel's enemies; the Qumran community saw a connection between the Servant and their own sufferings. Philip's answer — 'beginning from this scripture he told him the good news about Jesus' — is the interpretive tradition that identifies the suffering Servant as Jesus.
35: 'Beginning from this scripture' (arxamenos apo tēs graphēs tautēs): Philip uses Isa 53 as his hermeneutical starting point, but the gospel includes more than the single passage — he 'told him the good news about Jesus' comprehensively. The pattern is the same as Jesus with the Emmaus disciples (Luke 24:27): beginning with Moses and all the prophets, explaining the things about himself. The apostolic hermeneutic reads the OT as a whole as the anticipatory story whose protagonist is Jesus.
36: 'Look, water! What prevents me from being baptized?' (idou hydōr, ti kōlyei me baptisthēnai): the eunuch's question introduces the key word kōlyō (prevent/hinder) that recurs in Acts' barrier-crossing episodes (Acts 10:47: 'can anyone withhold (kōlysai) water from these people?'; Acts 11:17: 'who was I that I could hinder (kōlysai) God?'). The eunuch's question anticipates and preempts the possible objection — his Deuteronomic exclusion as a eunuch (Deuteronomy 23:1) is the barrier that the gospel, in light of Isaiah 56:3-5, dissolves.
37: Verse 37 (Philip's confession requirement and the eunuch's response) is absent from the oldest and best manuscripts (P45, P74, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is a later scribal addition, probably inserted to supply the baptismal formula implied but not stated. The verse is an early liturgical interpolation showing how the church formalized the baptism narrative to include an explicit faith-confession. Its absence from the critical text does not affect the narrative: baptism follows immediately on the eunuch's question.
38: The chariot stopped, both Philip and the eunuch went down into the water, and Philip baptized him: the immersion is physically full — both going down into (katebēsan eis to hydōr... anebēsan ek tou hydōrou) and up from the water. The road from Jerusalem to Gaza ran through the Judean desert; there are several small streams and ponds along this route. The spontaneous baptism with road-side water mirrors Jewish proselyte immersion (also in water, also immersive) — the eunuch's conversion uses the ritual form that already existed for Gentile entry into Israel's covenant community.
39: The Spirit of the Lord snatched Philip away (pneuma kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon): harpazō (snatch/seize/transport) is the verb of supernatural relocation (1 Thessalonians 4:17 — the eschatological 'catching up'; 2 Corinthians 12:2-4 — Paul's heavenly vision). The eunuch 'saw him no more, and went on his way rejoicing' — his joy is the Lukan mark of salvation (cf. Acts 8:8; Luke 15). The narrative end is the eunuch's missionary return to Ethiopia: Philip's mission is complete with a single convert who carries the gospel to Africa.
40: Philip found himself at Azotus (Ashdod, the Philistine city on the coastal plain) and preached through all the towns until he came to Caesarea: the coastal strip between Azotus and Caesarea was a mixed Jewish-Gentile corridor with multiple small cities (Jamnia, Joppa, Antipatris, Caesarea). Philip's extended mission through this corridor connects the Samaritan and Ethiopian missions to his long-term base in Caesarea (Acts 21:8 — 'Philip the evangelist, one of the Seven'), where he will still be active decades later.
